questions.question: Explain the need for memory management in modern operating systems.
keyPoints: Multiprogramming requires sharing of limited physical memory among multiple processes
Memory management enables process isolation and protection
Optimizes CPU utilization by keeping more processes in memory
Enables virtual memory allowing programs larger than physical memory to run
Provides efficient allocation and deallocation of memory blocks

questions.question: Differentiate between internal fragmentation and external fragmentation with examples.
keyPoints: Internal fragmentation: allocated memory exceeds required memory due to fixed allocation units (e.g., allocating 8KB for a 7KB process in 4KB page system)
External fragmentation: free memory scattered in non-contiguous blocks (e.g., after many allocations/deallocations, no single block is large enough
Paging eliminates external but suffers from internal
Contiguous allocation suffers from both types

questions.question: Describe the three types of address binding in memory management.
keyPoints: Compile-time binding: absolute addresses known at compile time, program cannot be relocated
Load-time binding: addresses bound at load time, process can be loaded to any location but cannot move after loading
Execution-time binding: addresses bound during execution, process can be moved during execution (requires hardware support like MMU)

questions.question: What is the role of the Memory Management Unit (MMU)? Explain with its key functions.
keyPoints: Translates logical addresses to physical addresses using relocation register/base register
Provides memory protection by checking address validity against limit register
Enables virtual memory by handling page faults and page table lookups
May include TLB cache for faster address translation
Supports both paging and segmentation address translation

questions.question: Explain the concept of virtual memory and its advantages.
keyPoints: Virtual memory creates illusion of larger memory than physically present using disk storage
Allows programs larger than physical memory to execute
Increases degree of multiprogramming by keeping more processes in memory
Provides memory protection between processes
Simplifies programming by abstracting physical memory details

questions.question: Compare paging and segmentation memory management techniques.
keyPoints: Paging: fixed-size pages and frames, eliminates external fragmentation, internal fragmentation present
Segmentation: variable-sized segments matching programmer's view, both fragmentations possible
Paging offers faster address translation due to fixed-size
Segmentation provides logical protection and shared segments
Both can be combined for benefits of both approaches

questions.question: Explain the memory management techniques for a single-user single-tasking system versus a multiprogrammed system. How do the requirements differ?
keyPoints: Single-user single-tasking: simpler memory management, no need for protection between processes, may use contiguous allocation, swapping rarely needed
Multiprogrammed: requires memory management supporting multiple processes simultaneously
Needs process isolation and protection, memory sharing mechanisms
Uses paging/segmentation for non-contiguous allocation
Complex allocation strategies to minimize fragmentation
Demand paging and virtual memory for efficiency
OS must maintain page tables, handle page faults, manage free frames

questions.question: Discuss the various criteria and algorithms used for memory allocation in contiguous memory allocation. Explain the problems associated with contiguous allocation.
keyPoints: First-fit: allocate first hole large enough - fast but may cause external fragmentation
Best-fit: allocate smallest hole large enough - minimizes wasted space but slower
Worst-fit: allocate largest hole - leaves large leftover but rarely used
Problems: External fragmentation requiring compaction, internal fragmentation, limited flexibility
Fixed partition causes internal fragmentation, dynamic partitioning cause external fragmentation
Limited scalability as processes grow, complex relocation requirements

questions.question: Explain the concept of swapping in memory management. Discuss its advantages, disadvantages, and the modifications required in the process state for implementing swapping.
keyPoints: Swapping: moving entire process between memory and secondary storage
Process state modifications: all process information including registers, program counter, memory maps must be saved and restored
Advantages: allows more processes than physical memory can hold, enables process relocation
Disadvantages: slow transfer times, may cause thrashing if overused, complex state management
Swap space management on disk, maintaining swap maps
Modified versions: partial swapping (rolling) and demand paging as optimizations

questions.question: Describe the hardware support required for implementing memory management. Discuss base and limit registers, page tables, segment tables, and TLB.
keyPoints: Base register: holds starting physical address for relocation
Limit register: holds size of process memory for protection
Page table: maps page numbers to frame numbers, stored in memory
Segment table: base and limit for each segment, enables variable-sized segments
TLB: associative cache for page table entries, speeds up translation
MMU integrates all these functions for address translation and protection
Hardware must support different privilege levels for OS and user access
